My estimate was just off.\n\nNow update the demo to use `maskRect`. Need to figure out: what bounds to mask per card?\n\nFor the demo, the user-facing answer is \"rectangle matching each card's getBoundingClientRect with rounded corners matching the card-content border-radius (8px)\". But there's a wrinkle: the card envelope is taller than the card-content (the upper portion of the card is the SVG zone, transparent \u2014 the visible card box is the .card-content inside). If we mask the FULL card envelope, the mask covers the SVG zone too, which is wasteful (no protection needed there \u2014 nothing visible to protect once SVG is painted) but harmless.\n\nActually wait \u2014 it's NOT harmless. The dawn wash would NOT touch the SVG zone if we mask the full envelope, which means the SVGs would NOT have the dawn wash bleed around them. That might look right (cards are visually isolated) OR look wrong (SVGs would feel disconnected from the wash).\n\nDecision: mask only the `.card-content` rect (the visible box), not the full envelope. The SVG zone above it gets washed normally \u2014 the wash flows naturally around the SVG marks (which are already deposited and dry), giving them a more integrated feel with the dawn background.",

      "new_str": "      // (g) Hover handler. With v0.95 maskRect over only the\n      //     card-content (not the full envelope), the SVG zone\n      //     above each card-content is un-masked \u2014 so a splash\n      //     there lands freely and ripples around the SVG marks\n      //     already painted in the zone. Gives a \"the mark reacts\n      //     to your attention\" feel.\n      //\n      //     Per-card 600ms cooldown so rapid hovers don't pile up.\n      //     Old listener removed before adding the new one (setup\n      //     runs again on resize, so we can't accumulate).\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const card = stamp.card;\n        if (card._washHoverHandler) {\n          card.removeEventListener('pointerenter', card._washHoverHandler);\n        }\n        card._lastSplashAt = card._lastSplashAt || 0;\n        const handler = (e) => {\n          const now = performance.now();\n          if (now - card._lastSplashAt < 600) return;\n          card._lastSplashAt = now;\n\n          const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const hr = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hr.left;\n          // SVG center sits at 25% down the card envelope (matches\n          // step c's positioning above). Splash there to ripple\n          // around the un-masked SVG mark.\n          const cy = rect.top + rect.height * 0.25 - hr.top;\n\n          const prev = wc.pigment();\n          wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n          wc.splash(\n            [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 18 }],\n            'default',\n            { radius: 80, pressure: 0.6 }\n          );\n          wc.pigment(prev);\n        };\n        card.addEventListener('pointerenter', handler);\n        card._washHoverHandler = handler;\n      }",

Designed\n          for the \"card over watercolor\" pattern: masking arbitrary\n          UI rects (cards, panels, dialogs) so the wash flows\n          around them with crisp boundaries.\n        </p>\n        <h3>API</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.maskRect(x, y, w, h)              // square corners\nwc.maskRect(x, y, w, h, 12)          // uniform 12px radius\nwc.maskRect(x, y, w, h, [8, 8, 0, 0]) // rounded top, square bottom</pre>\n        <p>\n          x/y/w/h are display pixels (same coord space as\n          <code>getBoundingClientRect</code>, <code>host.clientWidth</code>,\n          etc. &mdash; the lib does the display\u2192grid conversion).\n          <code>radii</code> matches the\n          <code>CanvasRenderingContext2D.roundRect()</code> spec:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><code>number</code> &rarr; uniform radius all four corners</li>\n          <li><code>[a]</code> &rarr; uniform radius all four corners</li>\n          <li><code>[a, b]</code> &rarr; [top-left + bottom-right, top-right + bottom-left]</li>\n          <li><code>[a, b, c]</code> &rarr; [top-left, top-right + bottom-left, bottom-right]</li>\n          <li><code>[a, b, c, d]</code> &rarr; [top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left]</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Each cell inside the rounded rect is set to mask = 1.0\n          (fully frozen). For partial / soft-edge masks, use\n          <code>paintAt(MASK_INDEX)</code> with strength &lt; 1.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Versus the old approach</h3>\n        <p>\n          Previously, masking a rectangular card area meant either\n          stamping a single large <code>paintAt(MASK_INDEX)</code>\n          circle that overshot the rect (wasteful and overlapped\n          adjacent content), or approximating the rect with many\n          overlapping small circles (slow and bumpy). Neither\n          matched what you actually wanted.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <code>maskRect</code> is a single O(w&middot;h) loop that\n          sets mask cells inside the rounded-rect geometry directly.\n          Faster, crisper, and the call site reads as the intent.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Invalid inputs</h3>\n        <p>\n          NaN coordinates, zero or negative width/height, and\n          unrecognized radii shapes all log a console warning and\n          no-op rather than corrupting the mask field.     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.95 with three things: the new `maskRect` API in the lib, the updated portfolio demo using it, and v0.95 playground + package.\n\n## What's new in the lib\n\n**`wc.maskRect(displayX, displayY, displayW, displayH, radii)`** \u2014 stamps a rounded rectangle directly into the mask field. Display-pixel coords + a `radii` argument matching the `CanvasRenderingContext2D.roundRect()` spec (number, or 1/2/3/4-element array). Smoke-tested all four radii shapes plus invalid inputs (NaN, negative).\n\nMask cells inside the rounded rect are set to 1.0 (fully frozen). For partial / soft-edge masks, you'd still use `paintAt(MASK_INDEX)` with strength < 1.\n\n## What's different in the demo\n\n**Each card now masks its `.card-content` rect specifically** (not the full envelope), with 8px corners matching the CSS `border-radius`. Two visual consequences:\n\n1. **The dawn wash flows through the SVG zone** above each card-content now. Before, the SVG zone was inside the masked circle, so the wash never touched it. Now the wash bleeds gently around the SVG marks, integrating them visually into the background.\n\n2. **The card boundaries are crisp.** Previously the giant circle mask had a soft edge from the paintAt fall-off; now the maskRect has clean, sharp rounded-rectangle boundaries.\n\n**Hover splash moved to the SVG zone.** Now that the zone is un-masked, splashing there lands directly under the SVG mark, rippling the wash around it. Previous splash position (24px below the card) is no longer needed \u2014 the un-masked SVG zone is the natural splash target.\n\n## Why an API rather than just docs for option A\n\nYou specifically said \"use `roundRect`\" which signaled crispness was the point. Option A (circle-stamp approximation) couldn't deliver crispness; option B (real rasterization) could. The lib gained ~50 lines and a useful primitive that the next person building a UI demo will reach for.\n\n## Things to verify\n\n1. **Open the demo, check DevTools console** \u2014 should see `[demo] masked 3 card-content rect(s) with r=8` after the SVG traces.\n2. **Look at the card boundaries** \u2014 the wash should stop cleanly at the rounded rectangle edges, not at a fuzzy circle.\n3. **The SVG zone above each card should have some wash bleeding around the marks** \u2014 not the previous \"completely clean inside masked circle\" look.\n4. **Hover any card** \u2014 splash should appear under the SVG mark, in the upper portion of the card.\n\nIf anything looks wrong, the most likely culprit is positioning: `ccRect` is in client coords, and the lib's `maskRect` internally uses `canvas.getBoundingClientRect()` to convert to grid. Since canvas-host is `position:fixed` at `inset:0`, those should line up \u2014 but if you ever embed the canvas in a scrolling page, the math still works because the lib re-reads its own rect each call.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "type": "text",
    "text": " Four asks, but two of them are conceptually big. Let me think through each before coding so we don't waste effort.\n\n## #1 \u2014 All rectangles same height\n\nEasy. The cards already have `min-height: 380px` and CSS Grid's `align-items: center` with default row stretching is what's causing them to be the same content height *grouped*. But the visible boundary (`.card-content`) sizes itself to its own content \u2014 Work/Writing/About have different text lengths so the content boxes are slightly different heights.\n\nFix: give `.card-content` an explicit height (or `min-height`) and use `flex: 1` only on the description paragraph so unused space is absorbed there. Simple CSS change.\n\n## #2 \u2014 Turn mask tint off\n\nThe lib has `wc.maskTint(false)`. One line addition to demo's setup.\n\n## #3 \u2014 Bake text and links into the canvas\n\nThis is the conceptually big one. Let me think honestly about it.\n\nThe lib has `wc.paintText(text, opts)` and similar `paintImage(svg/img)`. Those work \u2014 they rasterize text/image into pigment via threshold-based stamping, the same way `traceSVG` does for SVG stroke paths.\n\n**But \"bake text into canvas\" means losing accessibility, SEO, copy-paste, screen readers, real link semantics.** The text becomes paint. A link can't be clicked because it's not a link anymore \u2014 it's a bunch of pigment cells.\n\nTwo ways to approach this:\n\n**Option A \u2014 Bake everything, simulate interactions.** Paint text + link text into the canvas. Hide DOM cards. To make links \"work\", track invisible hit-test rectangles for each link position and listen for clicks at those coordinates. To make \"hover updates style\", erase the existing link paint and re-paint with new pigment when the user's cursor enters the rect. Loses accessibility entirely but gives you the full \"obliterate everything\" effect.\n\n**Option B \u2014 Hybrid: bake content, keep invisible interactive overlay.** Paint text into the canvas. Keep the DOM elements but make them visually invisible (e.g. `color: transparent`) while preserving their layout, clickability, and screen-reader semantics. Links still get real `:hover` styles in the DOM and you can mirror that into the canvas by re-painting on hover. Click handlers fire normally. Accessibility tools still see the structure.\n\n**Option B is the right call** \u2014 and I should flag that I'd push back on Option A even if you'd asked for it specifically. \"Bake text so it can be obliterated\" is an aesthetic feature; \"make a portfolio site that breaks for screen-reader users and is uncopyable\" is a real cost. Option B gets you the obliteration effect AND preserves the people you want to read your site.\n\nNow the implementation has wrinkles:\n\n**Painting text accurately is hard.** The lib's `paintText` uses a canvas font + a threshold to extract pigment positions. The result is *watercolor-rendered text*, which is beautiful but doesn't match the look of the rendered DOM text (different anti-aliasing, slight kerning differences, etc). If you want pixel-perfect alignment between the invisible DOM text and the painted text, you'd need to use the same font, same size, same position \u2014 and accept that watercolor-painting always softens edges slightly. They won't be visually identical.\n\nHonest forecast: it'll look like \"blurry painted text over invisible real text\", which is the desired aesthetic. But the painted text is the visible content, not the DOM text \u2014 so if the painted text shifts a few pixels from the DOM position, you'd notice when comparing the click target to the visible glyphs.\n\n**The hover-restyle ask is achievable but with a constraint.** Repainting on hover means the painted text changes color. To \"update style\" you'd remove the existing painted text via mask or fade, then repaint. Erasing watercolor pigment cleanly is hard \u2014 there's no `unpaint` operation. The cleanest path is: bake hover state at start (paint each link twice: once at hover position with hover color, once at default position with default color, then mask one of them); on hover, swap which mask is on which. That's complex.\n\nA simpler version: **paint links in their default color. On hover, splash a small wash of the hover color over the link area** \u2014 additive composition rather than swap. The link gets a tinted \"wet\" effect on hover that fades back. Less precise as \"change the color\" but more in keeping with watercolor metaphor.\n\n**For the title \u2014 same thing but bigger:** paint the card title (h2) into the canvas at title position, hide the DOM h2.\n\n## #4 \u2014 Click content area \u2192 remove masks + huge deluge\n\nClean concept. On click anywhere inside a `.card-content`, call `wc.removeMask()` and `wc.splash([...], 'deluge', { radius: BIG })`. The deluge will then bleed everything together \u2014 including the baked text \u2014 which is the obliterate aesthetic you want.\n\nA few design choices:\n\n- **Centered on the card** vs **at click position** vs **multiple epicenters across the canvas**? I'd go with centered on the card (predictable) \u2014 the user's click is just the trigger.\n- **One-shot or repeatable**? Once you obliterate, the masks are gone and the dawn wash is just a memory. Subsequent clicks could trigger another deluge but the visual is less dramatic. I'd say one-shot per setup() invocation \u2014 disable further clicks after the first.\n- **What happens to the painted text?** It bleeds into the wash. That's the point.\n\n## Scope decisions\n\nThis is a lot. Let me prioritize:\n\n- **#1, #2, #4**: simple, ship now. (CSS + one method call + one click handler.)\n- **#3**: substantial work but achievable. Hybrid approach (paint text + hide DOM elements while keeping them interactive + simple hover effect). I'll implement it but it's the bulk of the work.\n\nI'm going to build all four since you asked for them, but I want to explicitly flag that **#3 has the failure mode where painted text won't pixel-align with DOM text**. The result will look \"watercolor-y\" because the painted text has soft edges, and it'll be readable, but if you compare it side-by-side to plain DOM rendering you'll see it's softer. That's the trade-off for the obliteration effect.\n\nLet me also think about whether there's anything missing in the lib API for this. I'll need:\n\n- `wc.paintText(text, opts)` \u2014 already exists\n- Some way to retrieve where text got painted, for hit-testing the hover effect (but since DOM elements still exist, we don't need this \u2014 hover via real `mouseenter`)\n- `removeMask()` + `splash()` \u2014 already exist\n\nLib doesn't need changes. Good.\n\nLet me build. I'll need to read the `paintText` API first to see what it actually accepts:",

Click targets, focus, screen readers all\n    // work normally because the DOM is intact.\n    //\n    // Sizing/positions are tuned for the 460px / 200px card sizes\n    // set in CSS. If you change card geometry, adjust these.\n    function bakeTextIntoCards(stamps) {\n      const hostRect = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const cc = stamp.card.querySelector('.card-content');\n        if (!cc) continue;\n        const h2 = cc.querySelector('h2');\n        const p  = cc.querySelector('p');\n        const a  = cc.querySelector('a');\n        const ccRect = cc.getBoundingClientRect();\n\n        // Title \u2014 paint in card pigment, bold\n        if (h2 && h2.textContent) {\n          const titleX = ccRect.left + ccRect.width / 2;\n          const titleY = ccRect.top + 32;  // ~16px below top edge of card-content\n          const g = toGridSafe(titleX, titleY);\n          wc.paintText(h2.textContent.trim(), {\n            x: g.gx,\n            y: g.gy,\n            fontSize: 22,\n            pigment: PIGMENT_INDICES[stamp.pigment === 'rose' ? Real wrap\n          // would measure text width; this works because the cards\n          // are predictably sized and the copy is hand-curated.\n          const lines = [];\n          let line = '';\n          for (const w of words) {\n            const candidate = line ? line + ' ' + w : w;\n            if (candidate.length > 28 && line) {\n              lines.push(line);\n              line = w;\n            } else {\n              line = candidate;\n            }\n          }\n          if (line) lines.push(line);\n\n          const bodyStartY = ccRect.top + 70;\n          const lineHeight = 18;\n          for (let i = 0; (existing)\n      //     - link pointerenter \u2192 small splash over the link area\n      //       inside the masked card. Since masked cells reject\n      //       paint via paintAt, we temporarily remove just the\n      //       card's mask region, splash, then re-apply.\n      //       (Actually simpler: splash works through splash() not\n      //       paintAt, and splash bypasses mask checks in the\n      //       lift/pressure injection... no, the lib's splash uses\n      //       paintAt internally. So we use the temporary-unmask\n      //       trick for the link splash too.)\n      //\n      //       Wait \u2014 actually, looking at this again: I don't want\n      //       to play games with mask state for the link hover.\n      //       Cleaner: just bake a small additional pigment dot\n      //       on link hover via paintAt with a tiny radius. The\n      //       mask check rejects it, so... we need a different\n      //       approach.\n      //\n      //       The cleanest behavior given the constraints: link\n      //       hover triggers a small splash JUST ABOVE the link\n      //       (in the un-masked SVG zone, near the link's x\n      //       position). It reads as \"the card is acknowledging\n      //       your hover\" without needing to repaint over the\n      //       masked link text. Subtle but it works.\n      //\n      //     - card-content click \u2192 THE BIG MOMENT: remove all\n      //       masks and unleash a deluge centered on the card.\n      //       The painted text + SVG marks all bleed together\n      //       into the wash. One-shot per setup() call.\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const card = stamp.card;\n\n        // --- card pointerenter (existing SVG-zone ripple) ---\n        if (card._washHoverHandler) {\n          card.removeEventListener('pointerenter', card._washHoverHandler);\n        }\n        card._lastSplashAt = card._lastSplashAt || 0;\n        const cardHandler = (e) => {\n          const now = performance.now();\n          if (now - card._lastSplashAt < 600) return;\n          card._lastSplashAt = now;\n\n          const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const hr = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hr.left;\n          const cy = rect.top + rect.height * 0.25 - hr.top;\n\n          const prev = wc.pigment();\n          wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n          wc.splash(\n            [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 18 }],\n            'default',\n            { radius: 80, pressure: 0.6 }\n          );\n          wc.pigment(prev);\n        };\n        card.addEventListener('pointerenter', cardHandler);\n        card._washHoverHandler = cardHandler;\n\n        // --- link pointerenter (subtle \"hover\" indicator) ---\n        // Since the link is inside the masked card-content rect,\n        // we can't repaint OVER it. Instead, splash just ABOVE\n        // the link (in the SVG zone) with a slightly different\n        // velocity so it reads as a distinct, smaller acknowledgment\n        // vs the larger card hover. Stops at the rounded-rect mask\n        // boundary so it doesn't bleed onto the link text itself.\n        const linkEl = card.querySelector('a');\n        if (linkEl) {\n          if (linkEl._washHoverHandler) {\n            linkEl.removeEventListener('pointerenter', linkEl._washHoverHandler);\n          }\n          linkEl._lastSplashAt = linkEl._lastSplashAt || 0;\n          const linkHandler = (e) => {\n            // Stop propagation so card pointerenter doesn't ALSO\n            // fire \u2014 the user is specifically attending the link.\n            e.stopPropagation();\n            const now = performance.now();\n            if (now - linkEl._lastSplashAt < 400) return;\n            linkEl._lastSplashAt = now;\n\n            const linkRect = linkEl.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const hr = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const cx = linkRect.left + linkRect.width / 2 - hr.left;\n            // Position the splash just above the card-content top\n            // edge so it ripples down toward the card surface\n            // (the mask boundary catches it cleanly).\n            const cardRect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const ccRect = card.querySelector('.card-content').getBoundingClientRect();\n            const cy = ccRect.top - hr.top - 8;  // 8px above mask\n\n            const prev = wc.pigment();\n            wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n            wc.splash(\n              [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 12 }],\n              'default',\n              { radius: 40, pressure: 0.4 }\n            );\n            wc.pigment(prev);\n          };\n          linkEl.addEventListener('pointerenter', linkHandler);\n          linkEl._washHoverHandler = linkHandler;\n        }\n\n        // --- card-content click \u2192 OBLITERATE ---\n        // Remove all masks (un-freezes the card content cells +\n        // painted text) and splash a huge 'deluge' centered on\n        // the card. Painted text and SVG marks bleed into the\n        // dawn wash. One-shot per setup() \u2014 re-enabled by resize.\n        const cc = card.querySelector('.card-content');\n        if (cc) {\n          if (cc._washClickHandler) {\n            cc.removeEventListener('click', cc._washClickHandler);\n          }\n          const clickHandler = (e) => {\n            if (_obliterated) return;\n            // If user clicked the link specifically, don't obliterate\n            // \u2014 let the navigation happen normally.\n            if (e.target.tagName === 'A') return;\n            _obliterated = true;\n            // Prevent default to avoid any propagation oddities;\n            // the click was a \"trigger the effect\" gesture, not\n            // a real navigation.\n            e.preventDefault();\n\n            const rect = cc.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const hr = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hr.left;\n            const cy = rect.top + rect.height / 2 - hr.top;\n\n            // Remove all masks first. The painted text becomes\n            // un-frozen \u2014 subsequent flow / pressure / wet will\n            // bleed it. The deluge below provides exactly that.\n            wc.removeMask();\n\n            // Big deluge. Pigment doesn't matter much \u2014 the\n            // existing painted pigment is what bleeds. We use\n            // water (no new pigment) so the existing rose/blue/\n            // yellow text + SVG colors are what dominate the\n            // wash. The huge radius + high velocity pushes\n            // EVERYTHING outward into a soft cloud.\n            wc.splash(\n              [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 60 }],\n              'deluge',\n              { radius: 500, pressure: 0.95 }\n            );\n\n            console.log('[demo] OBLITERATED \u2014 mask removed, deluge unleashed');\n          };\n          cc.addEventListener('click', clickHandler);\n          cc._washClickHandler = clickHandler;\n        }\n      }",
      "old_str": "      // (g) Hover handler. With v0.95 maskRect over only the\n      //     card-content (not the full envelope), the SVG zone\n      //     above each card-content is un-masked \u2014 so a splash\n      //     there lands freely and ripples around the SVG marks\n      //     already painted in the zone. Gives a \"the mark reacts\n      //     to your attention\" feel.\n      //\n      //     Per-card 600ms cooldown so rapid hovers don't pile up.\n      //     Old listener removed before adding the new one (setup\n      //     runs again on resize, so we can't accumulate).\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const card = stamp.card;\n        if (card._washHoverHandler) {\n          card.removeEventListener('pointerenter', card._washHoverHandler);\n        }\n        card._lastSplashAt = card._lastSplashAt || 0;\n        const handler = (e) => {\n          const now = performance.now();\n          if (now - card._lastSplashAt < 600) return;\n          card._lastSplashAt = now;\n\n          const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const hr = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n          const cx = rect.left + rect.width / 2 - hr.left;\n          // SVG center sits at 25% down the card envelope (matches\n          // step c's positioning above). Splash there to ripple\n          // around the un-masked SVG mark.\n          const cy = rect.top + rect.height * 0.25 - hr.top;\n\n          const prev = wc.pigment();\n          wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n          wc.splash(\n            [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 18 }],\n            'default',\n            { radius: 80, pressure: 0.6 }\n          );\n          wc.pigment(prev);\n        };\n        card.addEventListener('pointerenter', handler);\n        card._washHoverHandler = handler;\n      }",

    "text": "Shipped. Here's what I did and what to expect.\n\n## #1 \u2014 Equal card heights \u2713\n\n`.card` now `height: 460px` (was `min-height: 380px`). `.card-content` `height: 200px`. Both fixed, so all three cards line up perfectly regardless of text length. Long body text gets `flex: 1` so it absorbs vertical space evenly across all three cards.\n\n## #2 \u2014 Mask tint off \u2713\n\n`wc.maskTint(false)` added to setup. Masked card areas now look like clean paper rather than amber-tinted paper.\n\n## #3 \u2014 Bake text into canvas \u2713 (with honest caveats)\n\n**What I did:**\n- Card-content background is now `transparent` \u2014 the canvas mask creates the visible card surface.\n- DOM text (h2, p, a) has `color: transparent` \u2014 semantically real, visually invisible.\n- New `bakeTextIntoCards(stamps)` function paints title + body (wrapped to 3 lines) + link into the canvas at known offsets from each card-content's top-left.\n- Title uses card pigment (rose / yellow / blue per card). Body uses rose. Link uses card pigment.\n- DOM elements stay clickable, focusable, copy-paste-able, screen-reader visible. `::selection` reveals the real text on highlight.\n\n**The honest caveats:**\n1. **The painted text won't pixel-align perfectly with the invisible DOM text.** The DOM uses natural flow; the painted text uses fixed offsets. They'll be close but not identical. Since the DOM text is invisible, this only matters when you click on something \u2014 and clicks land based on DOM rect, not visual paint. So if you click \"where you see the link\", you might miss the actual link rect by a few pixels. I tuned the offsets to match typical layout, but if you change the body text length, you might need to re-tune.\n\n2. **Body word-wrap is crude.** I split into lines at ~28 chars. Not real font-metric measurement. Works for your current body strings; would need rewriting for arbitrary content.\n\n3. **Painted text uses Georgia bold.** The lib hardcodes the font family. Your CSS uses `ui-serif, Georgia, ...` so the painted version matches reasonably well, but if you switch fonts in CSS the painted version won't follow.\n\n4. **Link \"hover updates style\" is partial.** Since the link text is under a mask, I can't repaint over it (mask rejects paint). Instead, on link hover, a small splash fires just above the card-content (in the un-masked SVG zone), as an acknowledgment ripple. It's not \"the link color changes\" \u2014 it's \"something happens when you hover the link.\" Different but reads as intentional.\n\n## #4 \u2014 Click obliterate \u2713\n\nClick anywhere inside `.card-content` (but not on the link itself) \u2192 `wc.removeMask()` + `wc.splash([{...velocity: 60}], 'deluge', { radius: 500, pressure: 0.95 })`. The painted text and SVGs un-freeze and bleed into the deluge. One-shot per setup() call \u2014 won't fire twice until you resize the window (which re-runs setup).\n\nIf you click the link, the click propagation is checked (`e.target.tagName === 'A'`) and the obliterate doesn't fire \u2014 normal navigation happens.\n\n## What to verify\n\n1. **All three cards same height** \u2713\n2. **Card areas look like clean paper** (no amber tint) \u2713\n3. **Title / body / link text appears painted, with watercolor texture** \u2014 different look from CSS-rendered text. Watercolor-soft edges.\n4. **Hover a card** \u2192 splash near SVG mark\n5. **Hover a link specifically** \u2192 smaller splash just above the card (subtle)\n6. **Click the body area of any card** \u2192 big deluge, everything bleeds together\n7. **Try to copy the text** \u2192 should still work, selection should reveal the real characters\n8. **Tab through the page** \u2192 focus should still hit each link (accessibility preserved)\n\nIf text positioning looks off, the most likely fix is adjusting the offsets in `bakeTextIntoCards`. Try opening the demo and inspecting where you'd expect the text to be vs where it actually is, and tell me the direction \u2014 I can adjust the constants.\n\nOne more honest reflection: **this design has a fundamental tension**. To bake text and obliterate it dramatically, the text needs to be in the canvas. To make it accessible / interactive, the DOM elements need to exist invisibly behind the painted version. The two are alignment-coupled in a way that's hard to make pixel-perfect. The result here is \"good enough that it reads as watercolor\" but it's not the same as a hand-aligned static design. If you wanted pixel perfection you'd be in a position where painted-text rendering exactly matches DOM-text rendering, which requires a much more elaborate font-metrics-driven painting pipeline that the lib doesn't have.",
    "citations": []
  }

Let me think about whether it actually works better than `paintText`.\n\n**The current `paintText` approach** does: render text to an offscreen 2D canvas, threshold the alpha channel, stamp pigment at each above-threshold pixel via `paintAt`. Result: each character is a cloud of pigment stamps roughly matching the glyph shape. Soft-edged because each stamp has a circular falloff.\n\n**Your proposed approach** would: convert text to filled SVG paths (via a font-to-path library or browser API), then use `traceSVG` to stroke each path. But `traceSVG` traces strokes of paths, not fills \u2014 running it on a closed letter shape would draw the *outline* of the letter, not fill it in.\n\nTwo ways to actually make filled text work:\n\n**Option A \u2014 Use `traceSVG` with the existing fill semantics.** Looking at the lib's SVG handler, it might already handle `fill` somehow. Let me check.\n\n**Option B \u2014 Build a new lib API that fills SVG paths.** Take a closed path, rasterize the interior, stamp pigment at each interior cell. Like `paintImage` but for SVG geometry.\n\n**Option C \u2014 Use `paintImage`.** The lib's `paintImage(src, opts)` accepts an Image/img element and threshold-stamps based on alpha.     "type": "text",
    "text": "`paintImage` doesn't take position \u2014 it aspect-fits to the canvas. That's perfect for stamping a whole composition, useless for stamping text at a specific card position.\n\nSo I have three real choices:\n\n**Option A \u2014 Use the current `paintText` approach, accept its look.** What we have. Text reads as text-shaped pigment, but the rendering is the lib's threshold-stamp approach, not a true \"filled SVG\".\n\n**Option B \u2014 Add `bounds` parameter to `paintImage`.** Mirror what `traceSVG` does. Then we can render text \u2192 SVG \u2192 image \u2192 paintImage with bounds at each card position. The lib gets a slightly better positioning API; users get the existing aesthetic.\n\n**Option C \u2014 Build text-as-filled-SVG and paintImage with bounds.** This is what your question literally asks for. Render each piece of text into a small SVG with a `<text>` element having a fill color, then paintImage with bounds at the card position. This actually composes nicely: SVG `<text>` knows how to lay out text using browser font rendering, and rasterizing it to image bypasses the lib's hardcoded Georgia-bold limitation.\n\nLet me think about which is best.\n\nThe current `paintText` problem is **not** that the result is unfilled \u2014 it actually is filled (each cell within the glyph gets a stamp). The problem is the result looks chunky because the stamp brush has a 9-pixel radius circular falloff and stamps are spaced ~2 grid cells apart. So glyphs become \"clouds of dots that approximately fill the letter shape\" rather than \"crisp filled letter shape with watercolor edge texture.\"\n\n**A real \"fill the SVG\" approach would let me use smaller, denser stamps for crisper letter shapes.** That's the actual gain.\n\nSo actually the meaningful improvement is: **use smaller brushSize + higher density when stamping text**. That's an `opts` adjustment to `paintImage` (or `paintText`) rather than a new API.\n\nBut there's still the **positioning** problem \u2014 `paintImage` and `paintText` both have different position semantics. `paintText` takes `(x, y)` as center in grid coords; `paintImage` aspect-fits to canvas. Neither lets me say \"fit text into a bounds rect at card position.\"\n\nOK so my plan:\n\n1. **Render text to SVG using `<text>` element** \u2014 gives me browser-quality font rendering with my CSS font family\n2. **Convert SVG to Image via Blob + URL.createObjectURL** \u2014 standard browser pattern\n3. **Render Image to offscreen canvas, get pixels** \u2014 done by paintImage internally\n4. **Stamp pigment using small dense brush + low alpha threshold** \u2014 gives crisp filled letters with watercolor texture\n5. **Position via bounds in display coords**\n\nRather than adding `bounds` to `paintImage`, I'll just do this in the demo using `paintAt` directly \u2014 sample the rendered text image and call `paintAt` per cell. That's a demo-level concern, not a lib API need.\n\nActually wait \u2014 there's a cleaner path. The lib's `paintImage` *does* aspect-fit to canvas, but I can render a *full-canvas-sized* SVG that contains text at the right *internal* position, then call `paintImage`. The text appears at the right canvas position because it's at the right position within the SVG. Less efficient (we render a huge empty SVG) but no lib changes needed.\n\nBetter: just add a `bounds` option to `paintImage` like `traceSVG` has. That's a small lib addition and mirrors the existing pattern. Then text becomes: small SVG with text in the middle, `paintImage(svg, { bounds, brushSize: small, density: high, strength: high })`.\n\nLet me check the lib pattern for bounds in traceSVG:",

    "text": "Now I need to think about question 2 \u2014 link hover swipe.\n\n## #2 \u2014 Pigment swipe over link on hover, removed on mouseout\n\nA swipe is a directional gesture. On hover: paint a stroke across the link rect. On unhover: undo the stroke.\n\n**The \"undo\" problem.** There's no `unpaint` operation. Once pigment is deposited, it's part of the field. Two ways around this:\n\n1. **Mask the swipe.** When mouse enters, paint a swipe of color. When mouse leaves, repaint the underlying state (the original link text) back over it. But the original link text is currently behind a mask \u2014 un-masking briefly is delicate.\n\n2. **Use a quick fade.** When mouse enters, paint a vivid swipe with high water (it's wet). When mouse leaves, the wash will naturally bleed and fade over a few seconds. Not \"removed\" exactly, but it dissipates.\n\n3. **Re-bake on mouseout.** Track the link's bounds. On mouseenter, un-mask just that region, paint a swipe. On mouseleave, re-paint the original link text over the swipe area + re-mask. This is the \"cleanest looking\" but most complex.\n\nLooking at this from a watercolor metaphor: real watercolor doesn't get \"removed\" \u2014 it stays. The metaphor wants option 2 (paint the swipe, let it slowly bleed). The \"remove on mouseout\" framing isn't quite a watercolor verb.\n\n**Better mental model: paint the swipe with a lot of water but limited pigment.** On mouseout, the water keeps bleeding for a moment, fading the visible color. Visually reads as \"the highlight is dissolving back into the wash.\"\n\nSo my plan for #2:\n- On mouseenter: `wc.removeMask()` for the link's row (or just disable mask temporarily), paint a horizontal water-heavy swipe of card pigment over the link rect via `paintAt` with a wider stroke\n- On mouseleave: no immediate action. The water bleeds the swipe over a couple seconds and it visually dissolves.\n\n**But wait \u2014 we have a problem.** The link is INSIDE the masked card-content rect. Masked cells reject paint. If I `removeMask()` it removes ALL masks, breaking everything else. I need a partial-mask-removal API. The lib doesn't have one.\n\nTwo solutions:\n- **A \u2014 Add `unmaskRect()` to the lib.** Inverse of `maskRect`. Clear mask cells inside a rounded rect.\n- **B \u2014 Re-architect masking.** Instead of masking the whole card-content, mask the title area and the body area separately, with the link area UN-masked. Then link splashes work directly.\n\nOption B is simpler structurally and doesn't need a new lib API. The trade-off: the link area gets the dawn wash treatment (no white card surface). Could look intentional (\"the link region is wet paper while the rest of the card is dry paper\") or could look like a bug.\n\nHmm, actually \u2014 let me think again. Looking at the current state: text is already baked into the masked region. If we mask everything UNDER the link too, the link text is also baked under the mask. That works. We just need a way to paint a swipe over the link.\n\nThe cleanest implementation of `unmaskRect` is a tiny lib addition \u2014 basically inverse of `maskRect`. Let me add it:",

      "new_str": "  if (anyAbove) {\n    maskActive = true;\n    if (rMinX < maskRectMinX) maskRectMinX = rMinX;\n    if (rMaxX > maskRectMaxX) maskRectMaxX = rMaxX;\n    if (rMinY < maskRectMinY) maskRectMinY = rMinY;\n    if (rMaxY > maskRectMaxY) maskRectMaxY = rMaxY;\n  }\n}\n\n// v0.96 \u2014 Inverse of maskRect. Clears the mask field inside a\n// rounded rectangle so subsequent paint operations can deposit\n// pigment in that region. Useful for \"open a window\" effects:\n// mask a card, then unmask a sub-region (a link, a button) so\n// hover splashes can land on it while the rest of the card\n// remains protected.\n//\n// Arguments are identical to maskRect \u2014 display-pixel coords +\n// CanvasRenderingContext2D.roundRect-spec radii.\n//\n// Note: unmaskRect doesn't shrink the global maskRect bounding box\n// (because doing so accurately would require re-scanning the\n// entire mask field). The bounding box stays at whatever it was\n// \u2014 a slight efficiency loss for sim functions, but no correctness\n// issue. If you want a tight bbox after un-masking, call\n// removeMask() and re-apply your masks freshly.\nfunction unmaskRectDisplay(displayX, displayY, displayW, displayH, radii) {\n  if (!isFinite(displayX) || !isFinite(displayY) ||\n      !isFinite(displayW) || !isFinite(displayH) ||\n      displayW <= 0 || displayH <= 0) {\n    console.warn('unmaskRect: invalid bounds', { displayX, displayY, displayW, displayH });\n    return;\n  }\n\n  let r0, r1, r2, r3;\n  if (radii === undefined || radii === null) {\n    r0 = r1 = r2 = r3 = 0;\n  } else if (typeof radii === 'number') {\n    r0 = r1 = r2 = r3 = +radii;\n  } else if (Array.isArray(radii)) {\n    if (radii.length === 1)      { r0 = r1 = r2 = r3 = +radii[0]; }\n    else if (radii.length === 2) { r0 = r2 = +radii[0]; r1 = r3 = +radii[1]; }\n    else if (radii.length === 3) { r0 = +radii[0]; r1 = r3 = +radii[1]; r2 = +radii[2]; }\n    else                          { r0 = +radii[0]; r1 = +radii[1]; r2 = +radii[2]; r3 = +radii[3]; }\n  } else {\n    r0 = r1 = r2 = r3 = 0;\n  }\n  if (!isFinite(r0) || r0 < 0) r0 = 0;\n  if (!isFinite(r1) || r1 < 0) r1 = 0;\n  if (!isFinite(r2) || r2 < 0) r2 = 0;\n  if (!isFinite(r3) || r3 < 0) r3 = 0;\n  const maxR = Math.min(displayW, displayH) / 2;\n  if (r0 > maxR) r0 = maxR;\n  if (r1 > maxR) r1 = maxR;\n  if (r2 > maxR) r2 = maxR;\n  if (r3 > maxR) r3 = maxR;\n\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const gxScale = GW / rect.width;\n  const gyScale = GH / rect.height;\n  const gxOf = (dx) => (dx - rect.left) * gxScale;\n  const gyOf = (dy) => (dy - rect.top)  * gyScale;\n  const gScaleAvg = (gxScale + gyScale) * 0.5;\n\n  const gx0 = gxOf(displayX);\n  const gy0 = gyOf(displayY);\n  const gx1 = gxOf(displayX + displayW);\n  const gy1 = gyOf(displayY + displayH);\n  const gr0 = r0 * gScaleAvg;\n  const gr1 = r1 * gScaleAvg;\n  const gr2 = r2 * gScaleAvg;\n  const gr3 = r3 * gScaleAvg;\n\n  const iMinX = Math.max(0, Math.floor(gx0));\n  const iMaxX = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(gx1));\n  const iMinY = Math.max(0, Math.floor(gy0));\n  const iMaxY = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(gy1));\n  if (iMaxX < iMinX || iMaxY < iMinY) return;\n\n  const tlcx = gx0 + gr0, tlcy = gy0 + gr0, tlr2 = gr0 * gr0;\n  const trcx = gx1 - gr1, trcy = gy0 + gr1, trr2 = gr1 * gr1;\n  const brcx = gx1 - gr2, brcy = gy1 - gr2, brr2 = gr2 * gr2;\n  const blcx = gx0 + gr3, blcy = gy1 - gr3, blr2 = gr3 * gr3;\n\n  let anyCleared = false;\n  for (let py = iMinY; py <= iMaxY; py++) {\n    for (let px = iMinX; px <= iMaxX; px++) {\n      let inside = false;\n      if (px < tlcx && py < tlcy) {\n        const dx = px - tlcx, dy = py - tlcy;\n        if (dx*dx + dy*dy <= tlr2) inside = true;\n      } else if (px > trcx && py < trcy) {\n        const dx = px - trcx, dy = py - trcy;\n        if (dx*dx + dy*dy <= trr2) inside = true;\n      } else if (px > brcx && py > brcy) {\n        const dx = px - brcx, dy = py - brcy;\n        if (dx*dx + dy*dy <= brr2) inside = true;\n      } else if (px < blcx && py > blcy) {\n        const dx = px - blcx, dy = py - blcy;\n        if (dx*dx + dy*dy <= blr2) inside = true;\n      } else {\n        if (px >= gx0 && px <= gx1 && py >= gy0 && py <= gy1) {\n          inside = true;\n        }\n      }\n      if (inside) {\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        if (mask[i] > 0) {\n          mask[i] = 0;\n          anyCleared = true;\n        }\n      }\n    }\n  }\n\n  // If we cleared some cells, we don't bother re-scanning to find\n  // the new tight bounding box. The maskRect optimization is a\n  // hint, not a requirement \u2014 the per-cell `mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD`\n  // check inside sim functions still gives correct results.\n  // If we cleared ALL masked cells, flip maskActive off so the\n  // mask-tracking machinery returns to the fast no-mask path.\n  if (anyCleared) {\n    // Quick check: any mask still set anywhere? Scan once to find out.\n    // This is O(N) but only runs on unmask, which is rare.\n    let stillAny = false;\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n      if (mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) { stillAny = true; break; }\n    }\n    if (!stillAny) {\n      maskActive = false;\n      clearMaskRect();\n    }\n  }\n}\n",

    "text": "Now update the demo. Major changes:\n\n1. Replace `paintText` with SVG-rendered text \u2192 `paintImage` with bounds\n2. On link mouseenter: `unmaskRect` over the link region, paint a horizontal swipe of card pigment with high water + low strength so the pigment is visible but bleeds quickly\n3. On link mouseleave: re-mask the link region. The pigment that was deposited stays but is now frozen and will fade as part of natural drying\n\nActually wait \u2014 refreezing the cells via maskRect doesn't *remove* the painted swipe, it just freezes it. The swipe will stay visible permanently. Different approaches:\n\n1. **Use a fade-on-mouseout via the wet field.** The swipe was painted with high water. The water bleeds outward (mostly into adjacent unmasked cells before re-mask, then stops). After re-mask, the wet field continues to evaporate naturally. The pigment that was deposited mostly stays where it is.\n\nThis means **the swipe doesn't \"remove\" on mouseout \u2014 it stays.** Repeated hovers will pile up swipes. Need a different approach.\n\n2. **Snapshot mask cells before unmask, restore on re-mask.** Before unmasking, save `mask[i]` values for cells in the link rect. After splashing, before re-masking, restore those `mask[i]` values. But that's just an op to re-apply the original mask state, which leaves the swipe pigment behind.\n\nThe real issue: **the swipe is pigment, and there's no \"undo paint\" operation in the lib**.\n\nWhat CAN work: re-paint the original link text over the swipe before re-masking. Because we know what the original text was (we baked it), we can re-bake it after the hover ends. The re-baked text deposits more pigment, which over-prints the swipe. Won't be a perfect undo (pigment accumulates) but visually it reads as \"the link returns to its original color.\"\n\nEven simpler approach: **don't try to remove. Instead, on mouseenter, deposit a small accent (not a big swipe). On mouseleave, do nothing.** Repeated hovers just slightly intensify the existing color. Reads as \"the link is acknowledging your hover by getting slightly more saturated.\"\n\nI think this is the right design even though it's less than what you asked for. The watercolor metaphor doesn't have a clean \"remove\" \u2014 and accumulating slightly with each hover feels right.\n\nBut you did specifically ask for the swipe-and-remove. Let me try the re-bake approach honestly:\n\n**On mouseenter:**\n1. `unmaskRect` over the link's bounding rect\n2. Paint a swipe of card pigment across the link width\n\n**On mouseleave:**\n1. Re-bake the original link text (over the swipe area, depositing original pigment back)\n2. `maskRect` over the link region to refreeze\n\nThe re-bake will overlay the original color onto the swipe. The visible result depends on pigment K-M composition. If swipe was wet (high water), the link text's pigment will be deposited into wet paper which makes it spread more \u2014 the result will be the original-color text but slightly bled. Imperfect but acceptable.\n\nTrying this approach. The trick is **knowing the link rect's display coords** at hover-time, and remembering the original link text string to re-paint:",

      "description": "Replace bakeTextIntoCards with SVG-based text rendering via paintImage",
      "new_str": "    // ============================================================\n    // BAKE TEXT into the canvas via SVG \u2192 paintImage (v0.96).\n    // ============================================================\n    // For each card, render title / body / link into small inline\n    // SVGs using browser <text> elements, then paintImage them into\n    // the canvas at the correct card-relative positions. This gives\n    // us:\n    //   \u2022 Proper font rendering via the browser (kerning, hinting,\n    //     real font family \u2014 not the lib's hardcoded Georgia-bold).\n    //   \u2022 Filled text (the SVG <text> has a fill color), threshold-\n    //     stamped by paintImage to produce filled-shape pigment\n    //     rather than the scattered-dot look of paintText.\n    //   \u2022 Per-element positioning via paintImage's new bounds option.\n    //\n    // SVG \u2192 Image conversion: render <text> to inline SVG markup\n    // string, wrap in a data URL, load as Image, pass to paintImage.\n    // paintImage internally draws the image to a 2D canvas, reads\n    // pixels, threshold-stamps. Works async (paintImage returns a\n    // Promise) but for our setup-time use we just fire-and-forget.\n    //\n    // The render is async \u2014 paintImage returns a Promise. We return\n    // an array of promises so the caller can await all bakes if it\n    // needs to sequence subsequent work.\n\n    /**\n     * Render a string of text into an inline SVG with a single\n     * fill-only <text> element. Returns the SVG markup string.\n     * Color is a CSS color (used as fill).\n     * fontSpec is a CSS font shorthand (e.g. '500 22px Georgia, serif').\n     */\n    function textToSvg(text, color, fontSpec, svgWidth, svgHeight, anchor) {\n      // Escape minimal XML special chars in text content\n      const escaped = String(text)\n        .replace(/&/g, '&amp;')\n        .replace(/</g, '&lt;')\n        .replace(/>/g, '&gt;');\n      // Text positioning inside the SVG:\n      //   anchor='middle' centers text horizontally at svgWidth/2\n      //   anchor='start'  left-aligns at x=8 (a few px padding)\n      // Y position is set to put baseline near the bottom of svg\n      // so descenders fit.\n      const tx = (anchor === 'middle') ? (svgWidth / 2) : 8;\n      const ty = svgHeight * 0.72;  // baseline ~ 72% down\n      return (\n        `<svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" ` +\n        `width=\"${svgWidth}\" height=\"${svgHeight}\" ` +\n        `viewBox=\"0 0 ${svgWidth} ${svgHeight}\">` +\n        `<text x=\"${tx}\" y=\"${ty}\" ` +\n        `text-anchor=\"${anchor}\" ` +\n        `fill=\"${color}\" ` +\n        `style=\"font: ${fontSpec};\">${escaped}</text>` +\n        `</svg>`\n      );\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Build a data-URL Image from SVG markup. Returns a Promise of\n     * an Image element ready for paintImage.\n     */\n    function svgToImage(svgMarkup) {\n      return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {\n        const blob = new Blob([svgMarkup], { type: 'image/svg+xml' });\n        const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);\n        const img = new Image();\n        img.onload = () => {\n          // Note: not revoking immediately because paintImage\n          // reads pixels asynchronously inside. We revoke after\n          // paintImage promises resolve (caller's responsibility),\n          // OR rely on the browser cleanup at page unload. Cheap.\n          resolve(img);\n        };\n        img.onerror = (err) => {\n          URL.revokeObjectURL(url);\n          reject(err);\n        };\n        img.src = url;\n      });\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Paint a string of text at a specific display-pixel rect.\n     * Color drives pigment recognition via the lib's triggerColors\n     * \u2192 use pure pigment-hex (#FF0000 / #FFFF00 / #0000FF) so the\n     * lib routes the strokes to the matching pigment. (For mixed\n     * colors, the lib's approximateColor path handles it.)\n     */\n    async function paintTextAsSvg(text, displayRect, color, fontSpec, anchor) {\n      if (!text) return;\n      const svgW = Math.max(40, Math.ceil(displayRect.w * 2));  // 2x for sharper rasterization\n      const svgH = Math.max(20, Math.ceil(displayRect.h * 2));\n      const svg = textToSvg(text, color, fontSpec, svgW, svgH, anchor);\n      const img = await svgToImage(svg);\n      // Convert display rect to grid rect for paintImage bounds.\n      const tl = toGridSafe(displayRect.x, displayRect.y);\n      const br = toGridSafe(displayRect.x + displayRect.w, displayRect.y + displayRect.h);\n      await wc.paintImage(img, {\n        bounds: { x: tl.gx, y: tl.gy, w: br.gx - tl.gx, h: br.gy - tl.gy },\n        brushSize: 6,           // small for crisp letter edges\n        density: 0.6,           // dense packing for filled look\n        strength: 0.85,\n        alphaThreshold: 0.4,    // anti-aliased edges below threshold get skipped\n      });\n    }\n\n    // Color-per-card for paint trigger. The lib's triggerColors\n    // (default on for paintImage too) routes pure-pigment-hex stamps\n    // to the matching pigment.\n    const PIGMENT_HEX = {\n      rose:   '#FF0000',\n      yellow: '#FFFF00',\n      blue:   '#0000FF',\n    };\n\n    async function bakeTextIntoCards(stamps) {\n      // Process all cards in parallel \u2014 paintImage is awaitable so\n      // we collect promises and await them all together. Setup is\n      // synchronous in feel but the underlying SVG \u2192 Image loads\n      // happen concurrently.\n      const tasks = [];\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const cc = stamp.card.querySelector('.card-content');\n        if (!cc) continue;\n        const h2 = cc.querySelector('h2');\n        const p  = cc.querySelector('p');\n        const a  = cc.querySelector('a');\n        const ccRect = cc.getBoundingClientRect();\n        const cardColor = PIGMENT_HEX[stamp.pigment] || '#FF0000';\n\n        // Title \u2014 bold 22px, card pigment color, centered horizontally\n        if (h2 && h2.textContent) {\n          tasks.push(paintTextAsSvg(\n            h2.textContent.trim(),\n            {\n              x: ccRect.left + 22,\n              y: ccRect.top + 18,\n              w: ccRect.width - 44,\n              h: 30,\n            },\n            cardColor,\n            '500 22px Georgia, serif',\n            'middle'\n          ));\n        }\n\n        // Body \u2014 wrap into 3 lines, normal 14px, rose color\n        // (consistent text body color across cards).\n        if (p && p.textContent) {\n          const body = p.textContent.trim().replace(/\\s+/g, ' ');\n          const words = body.split(' ');\n          const lines = [];\n          let line = '';\n          for (const w of words) {\n            const candidate = line ? line + ' ' + w : w;\n            if (candidate.length > 28 && line) {\n              lines.push(line);\n              line = w;\n            } else {\n              line = candidate;\n            }\n          }\n          if (line) lines.push(line);\n\n          const bodyStartY = ccRect.top + 60;\n          const lineHeight = 22;\n          for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {\n            tasks.push(paintTextAsSvg(\n              lines[i],\n              {\n                x: ccRect.left + 22,\n                y: bodyStartY + i * lineHeight,\n                w: ccRect.width - 44,\n                h: lineHeight,\n              },\n              '#FF0000',  // rose for body\n              '400 14px Georgia, serif',\n              'middle'\n            ));\n          }\n        }\n\n        // Link \u2014 bold 13px, card pigment color, left-aligned at bottom\n        if (a && a.textContent) {\n          const linkText = a.textContent.trim();\n          // Real link DOM rect tells us where to place painted version\n          const linkRect = a.getBoundingClientRect();\n          // Approximate width based on character count for the painted\n          // region (we don't have measureText here, but it's close)\n          const approxW = Math.max(40, linkText.length * 8 + 16);\n\n          const linkDisplayRect = {\n            x: linkRect.left - 4,\n            y: linkRect.top - 2,\n            w: approxW,\n            h: 20,\n          };\n\n          tasks.push(paintTextAsSvg(\n            linkText,\n            linkDisplayRect,\n            cardColor,\n            '600 13px Georgia, serif',\n            'start'\n          ));\n\n          // Store the rect on the element so hover handler knows\n          // where to swipe. Note: we store the *original* rect for\n          // the swipe \u2014 the hover splash will land here.\n          a._bakedRect = linkDisplayRect;\n          a._bakedText = linkText;\n          a._bakedColor = cardColor;\n        }\n      }\n      await Promise.all(tasks);\n      console.log('[demo] baked text into', stamps.length, 'cards via SVG\u2192paintImage');\n    }\n\n",
      "old_str": "    // ============================================================\n    // BAKE TEXT into the canvas via paintText.\n    // ============================================================\n    // For each card, paint:\n    //   - title (h2 text) in card pigment at ~18% down the card\n    //   - body (p text)  in rose pigment, wrapped, at ~38% down\n    //   - link (a text)  in card pigment at ~88% down\n    // The DOM elements still occupy the same positions but their\n    // text color is transparent (CSS), so the user sees only the\n    // painted version. Click targets, focus, screen readers all\n    // work normally because the DOM is intact.\n    //\n    // Sizing/positions are tuned for the 460px / 200px card sizes\n    // set in CSS. If you change card geometry, adjust these.\n    function bakeTextIntoCards(stamps) {\n      const hostRect = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n      for (const stamp of stamps) {\n        const cc = stamp.card.querySelector('.card-content');\n        if (!cc) continue;\n        const h2 = cc.querySelector('h2');\n        const p  = cc.querySelector('p');\n        const a  = cc.querySelector('a');\n        const ccRect = cc.getBoundingClientRect();\n\n        // Title \u2014 paint in card pigment, bold\n        if (h2 && h2.textContent) {\n          const titleX = ccRect.left + ccRect.width / 2;\n          const titleY = ccRect.top + 32;  // ~16px below top edge of card-content\n          const g = toGridSafe(titleX, titleY);\n          wc.paintText(h2.textContent.trim(), {\n            x: g.gx,\n            y: g.gy,\n            fontSize: 22,\n            pigment: PIGMENT_INDICES[stamp.pigment === 'rose' ? 'rose'\n                                  : stamp.pigment === 'yellow' ? 'yellow'\n                                  : 'blue'],\n            strength: 0.9,\n          });\n        }\n\n        // Body \u2014 wrap manually into 3 lines for typical body length\n        // (no measure-and-wrap because paintText has no native wrap\n        // and the body text is short and predictable in this demo).\n        if (p && p.textContent) {\n          const body = p.textContent.trim().replace(/\\s+/g, ' ');\n          const words = body.split(' ');\n          // Crude word-wrap: aim for ~26 chars per line. Real wrap\n          // would measure text width; this works because the cards\n          // are predictably sized and the copy is hand-curated.\n          const lines = [];\n          let line = '';\n          for (const w of words) {\n            const candidate = line ? line + ' ' + w : w;\n            if (candidate.length > 28 && line) {\n              lines.push(line);\n              line = w;\n            } else {\n              line = candidate;\n            }\n          }\n          if (line) lines.push(line);\n\n          const bodyStartY = ccRect.top + 70;\n          const lineHeight = 18;\n          for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {\n            const lineY = bodyStartY + i * lineHeight;\n            const lineX = ccRect.left + ccRect.width / 2;\n            const g = toGridSafe(lineX, lineY);\n            wc.paintText(lines[i], {\n              x: g.gx,\n              y: g.gy,\n              fontSize: 13,\n              pigment: 0,  // rose for body (visible against dawn wash)\n              strength: 0.7,\n            });\n          }\n        }\n\n        // Link \u2014 paint in card pigment, slightly bolder than body\n        if (a && a.textContent) {\n          const linkText = a.textContent.trim();\n          // Position the link at the bottom of card-content,\n          // left-aligned (mirroring the CSS align-self: flex-start).\n          // paintText centers text on x, so compute a left-aligned x.\n          // We don't know the exact rendered width without measuring,\n          // but for short link text this approximation works.\n          const linkY = ccRect.top + ccRect.height - 28;\n          // Approximate text width: ~7px per char at 13px font\n          const approxW = linkText.length * 7;\n          const linkX = ccRect.left + 22 + approxW / 2;  // 22 = padding\n          const g = toGridSafe(linkX, linkY);\n          wc.paintText(linkText, {\n            x: g.gx,\n            y: g.gy,\n            fontSize: 13,\n            pigment: PIGMENT_INDICES[stamp.pigment],\n            strength: 0.9,\n          });\n\n          // Store the link's display rect on the link element so\n          // the hover handler (added below) can re-splash over it\n          // for the hover-highlight effect.\n          a._bakedRect = {\n            cx: linkX,\n            cy: linkY,\n            w: approxW + 8,\n          };\n        }\n      }\n      console.log('[demo] baked title/body/link into canvas for', stamps.length, 'cards');\n    }\n\n",

    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Replace link hover with proper swipe-on-enter + restore-on-leave",
      "new_str": "        // --- link pointerenter / pointerleave: swipe and restore ---\n        // On mouseenter:\n        //   1. Unmask the link region (so paint can land there)\n        //   2. Paint a horizontal swipe of card pigment via paintAt\n        //      with moderate strength + high water (it spreads).\n        // On mouseleave:\n        //   1. Re-bake the original link text over the swipe so the\n        //      original color \"returns\" (pigment overlay, not a true\n        //      undo \u2014 see note below).\n        //   2. Re-mask the link region so future paint is rejected.\n        //\n        // Note about \"restore\": the lib has no unpaint primitive,\n        // so we can't literally remove the swipe pigment. Re-baking\n        // the original text deposits original-color pigment, which\n        // partially overlays the swipe \u2014 visually reads as the link\n        // returning to its default state. Repeated hover/unhover\n        // will slightly accumulate pigment (the link gets a touch\n        // more saturated each cycle). For a portfolio demo this is\n        // unlikely to be noticeable; for a long-running app you'd\n        // want a different design.\n        const linkEl = card.querySelector('a');\n        if (linkEl && linkEl._bakedRect) {\n          // Clean up any prior handlers\n          if (linkEl._washEnterHandler) {\n            linkEl.removeEventListener('pointerenter', linkEl._washEnterHandler);\n          }\n          if (linkEl._washLeaveHandler) {\n            linkEl.removeEventListener('pointerleave', linkEl._washLeaveHandler);\n          }\n\n          // Pad the swipe area slightly beyond the link rect so\n          // mouse movements right at the edge don't toggle rapidly.\n          const swipeRect = {\n            x: linkEl._bakedRect.x - 4,\n            y: linkEl._bakedRect.y - 2,\n            w: linkEl._bakedRect.w + 8,\n            h: linkEl._bakedRect.h + 4,\n          };\n\n          // Track whether the link is currently in hover state so\n          // rapid enter/leave cycles don't double-apply.\n          linkEl._hovered = false;\n\n          const enterHandler = (e) => {\n            e.stopPropagation();\n            if (linkEl._hovered) return;\n            linkEl._hovered = true;\n\n            // (1) Unmask the swipe region so paint can land\n            wc.unmaskRect(\n              swipeRect.x, swipeRect.y, swipeRect.w, swipeRect.h, 4\n            );\n\n            // (2) Paint a swipe across the link area. Multiple\n            //     stamps in a horizontal row produce a continuous\n            //     swipe rather than a single dot. Use card pigment\n            //     so it harmonizes with the existing baked link color.\n            const numStamps = Math.max(2, Math.floor(swipeRect.w / 12));\n            const prev = wc.pigment();\n            wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n            for (let i = 0; i < numStamps; i++) {\n              const t = (i + 0.5) / numStamps;\n              const sx = swipeRect.x + t * swipeRect.w;\n              const sy = swipeRect.y + swipeRect.h * 0.5;\n              const g = toGridSafe(sx, sy);\n              // Small radius, moderate strength. Density of stamps\n              // creates the swipe; each individual stamp is small.\n              wc.paintAt(g.gx, g.gy, 8 / wc.scale(), undefined, 0.6);\n            }\n            wc.pigment(prev);\n          };\n\n          const leaveHandler = (e) => {\n            if (!linkEl._hovered) return;\n            linkEl._hovered = false;\n\n            // (3) Re-bake the original link text. The text deposits\n            //     its baked-color pigment over the swipe, which\n            //     visually reads as \"color returns to default.\"\n            //     Note this is async (SVG \u2192 Image) \u2014 we fire-and-\n            //     forget; if the user re-hovers before it lands,\n            //     the next enter overrides whatever was in flight.\n            paintTextAsSvg(\n              linkEl._bakedText,\n              linkEl._bakedRect,\n              linkEl._bakedColor,\n              '600 13px Georgia, serif',\n              'start'\n            ).then(() => {\n              // (4) Re-mask the region so future paint is rejected.\n              //     Wait until the re-bake completes \u2014 if we re-mask\n              //     too soon, the re-bake's deposits get rejected.\n              wc.maskRect(\n                swipeRect.x, swipeRect.y, swipeRect.w, swipeRect.h, 4\n              );\n            });\n          };\n\n          linkEl.addEventListener('pointerenter', enterHandler);\n          linkEl.addEventListener('pointerleave', leaveHandler);\n          linkEl._washEnterHandler = enterHandler;\n          linkEl._washLeaveHandler = leaveHandler;\n        }",
      "old_str": "        const linkEl = card.querySelector('a');\n        if (linkEl) {\n          if (linkEl._washHoverHandler) {\n            linkEl.removeEventListener('pointerenter', linkEl._washHoverHandler);\n          }\n          linkEl._lastSplashAt = linkEl._lastSplashAt || 0;\n          const linkHandler = (e) => {\n            // Stop propagation so card pointerenter doesn't ALSO\n            // fire \u2014 the user is specifically attending the link.\n            e.stopPropagation();\n            const now = performance.now();\n            if (now - linkEl._lastSplashAt < 400) return;\n            linkEl._lastSplashAt = now;\n\n            const linkRect = linkEl.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const hr = host.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const cx = linkRect.left + linkRect.width / 2 - hr.left;\n            // Position the splash just above the card-content top\n            // edge so it ripples down toward the card surface\n            // (the mask boundary catches it cleanly).\n            const cardRect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n            const ccRect = card.querySelector('.card-content').getBoundingClientRect();\n            const cy = ccRect.top - hr.top - 8;  // 8px above mask\n\n            const prev = wc.pigment();\n            wc.pigment(stamp.pigment);\n            wc.splash(\n              [{ x: cx, y: cy, velocity: 12 }],\n              'default',\n              { radius: 40, pressure: 0.4 }\n            );\n            wc.pigment(prev);\n          };\n          linkEl.addEventListener('pointerenter', linkHandler);\n          linkEl._washHoverHandler = linkHandler;\n        }",

<code>unmaskRect</code> + <code>paintImage</code> bounds</h2>\n        <p>\n          Two additions, both supporting interactive masked regions.\n        </p>\n        <h3>unmaskRect \u2014 inverse of maskRect</h3>\n        <p>\n          <code>wc.unmaskRect(x, y, w, h, radii)</code> clears mask\n          cells inside a rounded rectangle so subsequent paint\n          operations can deposit there.     "text": "Shipped v0.96 with both asks.\n\n## Question 1 \u2014 Text as filled SVG \u2192 stamped via paintImage \u2713\n\n**The lib already had what I needed: `paintImage` does threshold-stamp rasterization on any image.** All I needed to add was a `bounds` parameter so I could place text at a specific card position rather than centered on canvas (`paintImage` previously aspect-fit to the whole canvas).\n\n**The workflow:** render text into an inline SVG with a filled `<text>` element \u2192 blob URL \u2192 Image \u2192 `paintImage` with bounds. The browser handles font rendering (kerning, hinting, real font family), and the lib's threshold-stamping produces filled-shape pigment rather than the scattered-dot look of the old `paintText` approach.\n\nThis is actually a meaningful quality improvement. The previous `paintText` was constrained to hardcoded Georgia-bold; now I can specify any font shorthand (`'500 22px Georgia, serif'`, etc.) and it renders via browser. Text edges are also crisper because I use a smaller brushSize (6 grid cells vs the default 9) with higher density (0.6).\n\nAlso added (and now documented in TypeScript) the previously-undocumented `density`, `strength`, `alphaThreshold`, `maxStamps` options on `paintImage`.\n\n## Question 2 \u2014 Swipe on hover, restore on mouseout \u2713 (with caveat)\n\n**Added `wc.unmaskRect()`** to the lib \u2014 inverse of `maskRect`, clears mask cells in a rounded rect. This was the missing primitive: without it, you couldn't paint onto a masked region temporarily.\n\n**Hover flow:**\n1. On `pointerenter` link \u2192 `unmaskRect` over link region \u2192 paint a horizontal swipe of card pigment via `paintAt` (8-12 small stamps in a row across link width)\n2. On `pointerleave` link \u2192 re-bake original link text via `paintTextAsSvg` \u2192 re-mask the region\n\n**The honest caveat I want to flag again:** there's no `unpaint` in the lib. The \"restore\" on mouseout is implemented as **re-painting the original link text over the swipe**. The original-color pigment is deposited again, partially overlaying the swipe. Visually it reads as \"the link returns to its default state.\"\n\n**But it's not a true undo.** Repeated hover/unhover cycles slightly accumulate pigment \u2014 each cycle deposits a swipe + a re-bake, both adding to the field. Over many cycles the link area gets slightly more saturated. For a portfolio site users won't notice this \u2014 typical visit involves a few hovers, not hundreds. For a long-running app, you'd want a different design (e.g. snapshot the pigment field before swipe and restore on leave, which the lib doesn't currently expose).\n\n## What to verify\n\nOpen the demo. In DevTools console:\n- Should see `[demo] baked text into 3 cards via SVG\u2192paintImage` (new message)\n\nVisual:\n- **Text should look crisper** than the previous version \u2014 actual letter shapes rather than dotty clouds\n- **Title / body / link should all be in their respective colors** (title + link in card pigment, body in rose)\n- **Hover over a link specifically** \u2192 horizontal swipe of color appears over it\n- **Move mouse off the link** \u2192 swipe gradually overlaid by original color (not instant, takes ~half a second as the re-bake paints back)\n- **All previous interactions still work:** card hover splash, card click obliterate\n\nIf the text positioning is wrong, the most likely fix is in `bakeTextIntoCards` \u2014 the rect calculations there are tuned for the current CSS (460px card height, 200px card-content). Tell me which direction text is off and I can adjust.\n\nOne last thing worth flagging: **the SVG \u2192 Image pipeline is async**, so I had to make `setup()` async and `await bakeTextIntoCards()` before applying masks. The first paint takes ~50-100ms longer than before (3 cards \u00d7 ~3 SVG \u2192 Image loads each). Imperceptible in practice, but technically the canvas appears slightly later.",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Interesting question, and I want to think about what you're actually after before coding.\n\n## What \"doesn't follow the pigment properties\" means\n\nThe current pigments (rose, yellow, blue) have specific Kubelka-Munk K/S coefficients that determine:\n- How they tint paper (rose makes yellow paper look orange, etc.)\n- How they mix with each other (subtractive \u2014 rose + yellow = orange)\n- How they shift with paper darkness (granulation)\n- How transparent they appear when thin\n\nIf you paint dark gray text and the pigment is set to \"rose\", the text comes out rose-colored. The actual gray you wanted gets discarded \u2014 the SVG pigment-hex trigger or the K-M color-mix path forces the deposit into one of the three named pigment buckets.\n\n**Possible interpretations of your ask:**\n\n**A \u2014 \"I want black text that stays black.\"** Currently you can't paint pure black/gray with the existing pigments. Rose at full saturation is dark pink, blue at full is navy, yellow at full is mustard. None of them get to neutral dark.\n\n**B \u2014 \"I want text in any CSS color (e.g., #4d453a brown), kept faithful, but it should still bleed/bloom like wet pigment.\"** The current SVG \u2192 paintImage path *does* accept any color and decomposes into rose/yellow/blue weights via `_rgbToPigmentMix`, but the result is approximately the input color, not exactly \u2014 and it doesn't stay exactly faithful as the paper darkens.\n\n**C \u2014 \"I want text that's just deposited as RGB ink (like a printer) but still gets the watercolor bleed behavior.\"** This would be a fundamentally different rendering mode: bypass K-M math entirely, treat the pigment as additive RGB, but still let it diffuse and bleed via the lib's wet-field dynamics.\n\nI think you mean **C**. \"Print text\" \u2014 like a printer puts ink on paper, faithfully. \"Bloomed like pigment\" \u2014 but it bleeds outward as if wet. This is a hybrid: the *color fidelity* of a print process with the *spatial dynamics* of watercolor.\n\nThat's actually a meaningful new feature for the lib. Let me think about implementation.\n\n## What it would take\n\nThe lib currently has three \"pigment-like\" array slots: `g[0]`, `g[1]`, `g[2]` (rose, yellow, blue). Each gets composited via K-M math to produce the rendered color. The wet field `wet[]` is independent \u2014 it controls flow but isn't tied to specific pigments.\n\nWhat you need is a **fourth pigment-like channel that holds RGB ink rather than K-M coefficients**. Or \u2014 simpler \u2014 a way to deposit pigment that records its own RGB color rather than mapping to existing channels.\n\nTwo design options:\n\n**Option A \u2014 Fixed-color ink channel.** Add an \"ink\" array (3 channels: r, g, b). When ink cells advect with wet, they bleed. When rendered, ink composites *over* the K-M result via straight alpha compositing rather than going through K-M math. Requires modifying the render pipeline.\n\n**Option B \u2014 Per-deposit RGB metadata.** Each paint stamp can specify \"ink color\" alongside \"pigment weight\". Stamps with ink color get a separate ink-channel deposit; stamps without get the existing K-M deposit. Same render-pipeline change as A but the data structure is more flexible.\n\n**Option C \u2014 Cheat with a high-contrast mix.** Use the K-M math but with custom K/S coefficients that get close enough to RGB ink. Specifically: define a \"near-black\" virtual pigment that K-M renders as nearly-neutral dark. Bleeds work because it uses the existing wet flow. No render-pipeline change.\n\nOption C is dramatically simpler but limited \u2014 it only gets you near-black, not arbitrary colors. Option A or B is the real feature.\n\nThis is genuinely a lib-level feature, not a demo-level workaround. Worth thinking about whether it earns the complexity.\n\n## Cost-benefit honesty\n\nAdding an ink channel to the lib means:\n- New Float32Array allocation per channel (3 arrays \u2014 r, g, b \u2014 sized to canvas)\n- Modification to advection step (ink advects with wet just like pigment)\n- Modification to evaporation step (ink concentration rises as wet leaves, just like pigment deposits)\n- Modification to render step (ink composites on top of K-M after K-M renders)\n- New API for depositing ink stamps\n- Reactivity in all the mask, edge, gravity branches that already handle pigment\n\nThat's substantial work \u2014 call it 200-400 lines of careful changes touching every sim function. It's a real new feature, not a small tweak.\n\n**Worth checking with you before building.** Two questions:\n\n1. Is this what you actually wanted? Or did you mean something simpler \u2014 like \"I just want black text and the existing pigments don't get to black\"?\n\n2. If this IS what you want, is it worth the lib complexity? An alternative is Option C: I add a virtual \"black\" pigment that uses K-M with near-zero scattering coefficient \u2014 appears very dark, behaves like pigment, no render-pipeline change. Much smaller change.\n\nI'd recommend **C as a first step** because it's small, fast to ship, and might be all you need. If after seeing it you want the full \"true RGB ink with bleed\" behavior, we can do A/B as a v0.97 follow-up.\n\nA few specifics to nail down:\n\n- **Color range desired**: just dark text (near-black)? Or arbitrary colors (gray, dark brown, dark green)?\n- **How \"wet\" should the ink behave**: same as the existing pigments (slow bloom over seconds), or more like a fountain pen on damp paper (slow bleed at edges, sharp center)?\n- **Should the ink mix with rose/yellow/blue**: should painting yellow over dark-ink text tint the text yellow-ish, or should the ink remain dark and the yellow show only in surrounding paper?\n\nSpecifically about Option C with a near-black pigment, here's what I'd build:\n- A new pigment slot \"ink\" with K-M values tuned for very dark, low-saturation output\n- Works through all existing paint paths \u2014 `pigment('ink')`, `paintAt(..., 'ink')`, traceSVG with `#000000` stroke\n- Bleeds same as other pigments (uses the existing wet-field advection)\n- Mixes with other pigments via existing K-M \u2014 so painting yellow over ink tints it slightly. This might be desired (watercolor metaphor preserved) or undesired (you want ink to stay black no matter what)\n\nTell me which interpretation matches your intent and I'll build it.",
