    "text": " Good \u2014 that's a clearer spec. Let me think through it carefully because there are some subtle interactions to get right.\n\n## What you're asking for, distilled\n\nA new \"ink\" pigment with these properties:\n\n1. **Renders as dark as possible** \u2014 near-black, maximum contrast against paper\n2. **High paint load by default** \u2014 saturates the cell immediately\n3. **Low water load by default** \u2014 minimal wet field deposited, so it doesn't bleed itself\n4. **Adjustable** \u2014 both loads can be overridden per-stamp\n5. **Crisp when deposited** \u2014 no immediate bleed; sharp letter edges\n6. **Blooms when external water is added** \u2014 when another wet pigment splashes onto ink, the ink advects with the wet field like any other pigment\n\nThe \"dry-then-mask\" workflow you mentioned makes sense: dry the paper aggressively before laying ink so the ink has nothing to bleed into, then mask the inked region so subsequent washes don't disturb it until you choose to.\n\n## Design decisions I need to make\n\n**1. How to make \"dark as possible\" with K-M.**\n\nK-M reflectance from a pigment with K (absorption) and S (scattering) is approximately `1 + K/S - sqrt((K/S)\u00b2 + 2K/S)`. To get near-black:\n- Very high K (absorbs all wavelengths) \u2192 low reflectance\n- Very low S (no light bouncing back) \u2192 low reflectance\n\nThe existing pigments (rose, yellow, blue) have moderate K and high S. For ink I'd want **extremely high K, very low S** \u2014 closest analog is real india ink or sumi ink.\n\nSpecifically: K \u2248 [8, 8, 8] (high absorption across all channels), S \u2248 [0.5, 0.5, 0.5] (low scattering). This gives a near-uniform dark gray that approaches black as concentration rises. The current pigments use K values around 0.5-2 and S values around 1-3.\n\n**2. Where to add the ink in the pigment array.**\n\nTwo options:\n- **A \u2014 Add to PIGMENTS array as index 3.** Clean, just another pigment. `wc.pigment('ink')` works the same way as rose/yellow/blue. The lib already has machinery to handle N pigments (the swatch UI iterates over them, etc.).\n- **B \u2014 Add as a special index like MASK_INDEX.** Like a separate channel with custom handling. More work, easy to mess up.\n\nOption A is the obvious right answer. The PIGMENTS array can grow.\n\n**3. How to handle the \"high paint load, low water load by default\" behavior.**\n\nThe lib currently has global `paintLoadMult` and `waterLoadMult` that apply uniformly to all paint operations. To get per-pigment defaults that override these, I have a few choices:\n\n- **C \u2014 Per-pigment multipliers.** Each pigment in PIGMENTS gets optional `paintLoadMult` and `waterLoadMult` overrides. When painting with that pigment, the per-pigment values are used instead of (or multiplied with) the globals.\n- **D \u2014 Per-stamp override.** Add `paintLoad` and `waterLoad` to the paintAt opts object. Caller supplies them or falls back to globals. The \"ink defaults\" are then a convention enforced at the caller site, not in the lib.\n\nC is more elegant \u2014 the ink \"knows\" about itself. D is more flexible but pushes the responsibility to the caller. I'd do C for the defaults and add D as a per-call override.\n\nBut wait \u2014 the existing PIGMENTS entries don't have per-pigment multipliers. Adding them touches the schema. Let me check:",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Now I understand the structure. Each pigment has `K`, `S`, `density`, `staining`, `granulation`. The lib has 3-pigment hardcoded loops in many places (`const p0 = PIGMENTS[0], p1 = PIGMENTS[1], p2 = PIGMENTS[2]`). That's a real architectural constraint.\n\nThis is a problem. **Adding a 4th pigment to PIGMENTS would require updating every site that loops \"for each of 3 pigments\"** \u2014 which is a lot. Render, advection, edge effects, GPU shaders, swatch UI, all use the hardcoded 3. The hardcoded triplet is baked deep into the codebase.\n\nLet me re-evaluate my plan. Three options:\n\n**Option X \u2014 Replace blue with ink.** Only 3 pigments, but one is \"ink\". You lose blue. Too destructive.\n\n**Option Y \u2014 Use index 0/1/2 dynamically.** Switch active palette like gouacheMode does. There's already PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT vs PIGMENTS_OPAQUE \u2014 could add PIGMENTS_INK with [rose, yellow, ink]. But you can't have ink AND blue simultaneously.\n\n**Option Z \u2014 Add ink as a special index like MASK/WATER.** Ink lives in a 4th channel that exists alongside the 3-pigment array. Render adds ink's contribution on top of the K-M result. Doesn't fight the hardcoded-3 architecture.\n\n**Option Z is the right design.** Let me reconsider \u2014 yes this matches my earlier \"Option B\" framing.\n\nActually, the simplest implementation of Z: **ink lives in its own Float32Array, treated like a pigment for advection/wet-coupling, but at render time it composites separately.** Render pipeline:\n1. Compute K-M reflectance from g[0], g[1], g[2] as usual\n2. Multiply by `(1 - ink[i] * alpha)` to darken in proportion to ink concentration\n3. Optionally add a tiny scattering term so very-dense ink isn't pure black (more realistic)\n\nThis adds one channel and one render-step modification. Doesn't touch the hardcoded 3-pigment loops at all.\n\nFor paint deposition:\n- `paintAt(gx, gy, r, 'ink', strength)` \u2192 deposits into ink[] instead of g[k]\n- Uses configurable ink-specific paint load and water load\n- Same advection: when wet flows, ink advects with it just like pigment\n\nFor advection: the lib's pigment advection moves `g[k]` cells along velocity vectors. I need to do the same for `ink[]`. Look at advection:",

One pass over the\\n    // active rect; ~N memory reads but no writes.\\n    let maxL1 = 0;\\n    const sy0 = Math.max(1, activeMinY);\\n    const sy1 = Math.min(GH - 2, activeMaxY);\\n    const sx0 = Math.max(1, activeMinX);\\n    const sx1 = Math.min(GW - 2, activeMaxX);\\n    for (let y = sy0; y <= sy1; y++) {\\n      const yo = y * GW;\\n      for (let x = sx0; x <= sx1; x++) {\\n        const i = yo + x;\\n        const s = Math.abs(u[i]) + Math.abs(v[i]);\\n        if (s > maxL1) maxL1 = s;\\n      }\\n    }\\n    const cflProduct = maxL1 * baseAdt;\\n    // v0.66 \\u2014 Cap raised from 16 to 64.     "type": "text",
    "text": "Lots of hardcoded loops. **But for ink, I don't need to add it to those loops** \u2014 ink is a separate channel that:\n- Gets its own advection step (separate function call alongside the existing pigment advection)\n- Gets its own evaporation deposit (or none \u2014 ink is ink, it doesn't get suspended/deposited like pigment)\n- Gets its own render pass that darkens the K-M result\n\nActually let me think simpler. **What does ink actually need to behave like?**\n\nYou want crisp text. You want it dark. You want it to bloom *when external water hits it*. So:\n\n- **Deposit ink** = mark a cell as \"has ink at concentration X\"\n- **Advection** = when wet field at that cell has velocity, the ink moves with the wet. Identical to pigment advection.\n- **No evaporation deposit** = ink doesn't get \"suspended\". It's just always at the cell, period. It can move via advection but it doesn't have a \"wet vs dry\" state distinction like pigment does.\n- **Render** = ink darkens the cell's reflectance in proportion to its concentration\n\nIf ink uses the SAME advection field as pigment (the velocity field driven by wet), then I just need to advect it whenever pigment advects. The cleanest way: make ink a \"fourth pigment\" but only for advection \u2014 extend the hardcoded 3-loops to be 4-loops when ink is active.\n\nThat's actually less invasive than I feared. The pigment loops at lines 1280, 1419, 1568 etc. are just `for k in 0..3`. Changing to `for k in 0..N` where N=3 or 4 depending on ink-active is a few lines.\n\nBut that touches a LOT of sites (10+ loops based on the grep). Let me think of an even simpler approach.\n\n**Cleanest minimal-touch design:** Ink is a separate `Float32Array ink[N]`. Has its own `advectInk()` function called from `simStep` right after `movePigment()`. Reuses the velocity field. Render adds an ink-darkening multiplier on top of the K-M output. New API: `wc.paintInk(gx, gy, gridRadius, paintLoad, waterLoad)`.\n\nThat's:\n1. New array (~7KB at 648\u00d7540)\n2. New advection function (~30 lines, copy-adapt from one of the pigment advection branches)\n3. New paint function (~30 lines)\n4. New render hook (~10 lines)\n5. New API methods\n\nAbout 150 lines total. Doesn't touch any existing 3-pigment loops because ink lives outside them.\n\nThis is the right design. Let me also think about the dry-then-mask workflow you mentioned.\n\n**Dry-then-mask flow for the demo:**\n1. Set evaporation very high (e.g. 200 \u2014 bone-dry)\n2. Wait a frame or two for wet to evaporate\n3. Paint ink (now there's no wet for it to bleed into; sharp deposit)\n4. Mask the ink region\n5. Restore evaporation\n6. Paint surrounding washes (mask protects ink)\n\nStep 2 (waiting) is the tricky one. In the current setup() everything is synchronous so \"wait a frame\" needs an `await new Promise(r => requestAnimationFrame(r))`. That's fine.\n\nBut wait \u2014 ink with `waterLoad: 0` would have minimal effect on wet anyway. If ink doesn't add wet, the dry-paper step is less critical \u2014 the ink itself isn't wet. The dry-paper step is only needed if ink uses some wet, which is the configurable case. By default with waterLoad=0, ink lands on whatever wet was there, and dries immediately.\n\nOK plan:\n1. **Add `ink[]` array** to lib\n2. **Add `advectInk()`** mirroring pigment advection, called after movePigment in simStep\n3. **Add `paintInk()` API** with high paintLoad default (1.0) and low waterLoad default (0.05)\n4. **Add ink render contribution** \u2014 darkens K-M output by `1 - ink[i] * INK_ALPHA`\n5. **Add ink to `removeMask()` / mask checks** \u2014 masked cells reject ink deposits, same as pigment\n6. **Update demo** to use `wc.paintInk()` for the title/body/link text\n\nLet me build.",

Lives outside the 3-pigment K-M system. Renders\n// as a darkening multiplier applied on top of the K-M reflectance.\n// Designed for \"print text on watercolor paper\" use cases: a third\n// medium that has its own crisp deposition profile but bleeds with\n// the wet field like the pigments do.\n//\n// Differences from a pigment:\n//   \u2022 Single channel (one Float32Array) \u2014 no per-RGB K/S because ink\n//     doesn't scatter, it absorbs uniformly across wavelengths.\n//   \u2022 No suspended/deposited split \u2014 ink is just \"amount per cell\".\n//     Doesn't go through the evaporation transfer that pigment uses.\n//   \u2022 Advects with the velocity field same as pigment (when external\n//     wet causes flow, ink moves with it \u2014 that's the \"bloom when\n//     other pigments hit it\" behavior).\n//   \u2022 Default paint load high (saturate immediately for crisp letters);\n//     default water load low (don't deposit wet alongside, so ink\n//     stays sharp by default).\nlet ink = new Float32Array(N);\nlet ink_tmp = new Float32Array(N);\nlet inkActive = false;  // gate render + advection cost when no ink\nconst INK_INDEX = -5;\n// Render-time alpha. ink[i] * INK_ALPHA is the fraction by which K-M\n// reflectance is darkened. With INK_ALPHA = 0.95, ink at concentration\n// 1.0 reduces reflectance by 95% (near-black). Tuned so that the\n// default paint load of 1.0 gives a clearly readable dark mark without\n// going to absolute zero (which looks like a hole, not ink).\nconst INK_ALPHA = 0.95;\n// Per-cell threshold below which ink is treated as zero (skip render +\n// advection cost on near-empty cells).\nconst INK_THRESHOLD = 0.005;\n\n// Masking fluid layer (v0.13) \u2014 one float per cell, 0 = clean, 1 = fully\n// masked. Caller's `strength` is the per-cell\n  // paint amount; the high `inkPaintLoadDefault` (1.0 by default) means\n  // a single stamp at default strength saturates the cell. The water\n  // load is intentionally low so default ink stamps don't bleed\n  // themselves \u2014 only when external wet (from pigment splashes) reaches\n  // the inked cells do they bloom via the shared velocity field.\n  //\n  // Per-stamp paint/water load can be overridden by setting the\n  // module-level defaults via wc.inkPaintLoad() / wc.inkWaterLoad().\n  if (pigmentIdx === INK_INDEX) {\n    const inkGain = inkPaintLoadDefault;\n    const wetGain = inkWaterLoadDefault * 0.55; // mirror the water-brush\n                                                 // baseline (which uses\n                                                 // 0.55 * waterLoadMult)\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - gx, dy = py - gy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        // Masked cells reject ink, same as pigment.\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        const falloff = 1 - Math.sqrt(d2) / r;\n        // Quadratic falloff for ink \u2014 gives a slightly more solid\n        // center than linear, helping crisp letter centers stay\n        // saturated when stamps overlap on a glyph stroke.\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        const add = strength * f2 * inkGain;\n        const newInk = ink[i] + add;\n        ink[i] = newInk > 1 ? 1 : newInk;\n        // Light wet deposit to keep advection coupling alive. Default\n        // load is low (0.05) so this is well below the pigment brush's\n        // 0.55 \u2014 letters stay crisp by default. User can raise the\n        // water load if they want self-blooming ink.\n        if (wetGain > 0) {\n          const ww = wet[i] + wetGain * falloff;\n          wet[i] = ww > 1 ? 1 : ww;\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    inkActive = true;\n    return;\n  }\n\n  // Water brush: no pigment added.       "new_str": "  unmaskRect(displayX, displayY, displayW, displayH, radii) {\n    unmaskRectDisplay(displayX, displayY, displayW, displayH, radii);\n    return api;\n  },\n\n  // v0.97 \u2014 Ink pigment. A separate single-channel layer alongside\n  // the K-M pigments. Renders as a darkening multiplier on top of\n  // the pigment-rendered color. Default high paint load + low water\n  // load: crisp deposits that bloom only when external wet pigment\n  // reaches them.\n  //\n  // Usage matches the regular pigment API:\n  //   wc.pigment('ink');\n  //   wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR);          // strength = current\n  //   wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'ink', s); // explicit\n  //\n  // Per-pigment load defaults (independent of paintLoad/waterLoad\n  // globals which apply to K-M pigments):\n  //\n  //   wc.inkPaintLoad(v?)  \u2014 default 1.0 (saturate immediately)\n  //   wc.inkWaterLoad(v?)  \u2014 default 0.05 (minimal self-bleed)\n  //\n  //   wc.clearInk()        \u2014 zero out the ink field everywhere\n  inkPaintLoad(v) {\n    if (v !== undefined) {\n      const n = +v;\n      if (!isFinite(n) || n < 0) {\n        console.warn('inkPaintLoad: expected non-negative number, got', v);\n        return inkPaintLoadDefault;\n      }\n      inkPaintLoadDefault = n;\n    }\n    return inkPaintLoadDefault;\n  },\n  inkWaterLoad(v) {\n    if (v !== undefined) {\n      const n = +v;\n      if (!isFinite(n) || n < 0) {\n        console.warn('inkWaterLoad: expected non-negative number, got', v);\n        return inkWaterLoadDefault;\n      }\n      inkWaterLoadDefault = n;\n    }\n    return inkWaterLoadDefault;\n  },\n  clearInk() {\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) ink[i] = 0;\n    inkActive = false;\n    if (useGPU) gpuRender(); else render(true);\n    return api;\n  },",
